coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots-embedded fluorescent carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogel for anticounterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Mogharbel, Amal T.
Hameed, Ahmed
Sayqal, Ali A.
Katouah, Hanadi A.
Al-Qahtani, Salhah D.
Saad, Fawaz A.
El-Metwaly, Nashwa M.
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent inks have been emerged as a desirable encoding technique to enhance anticounterfeiting printing of commercial goods. However, significant drawbacks with fluorescent inks, such as poor durability, low efficiency, and high cost. Herein, we describe the preparation of a self-healing authentication ink based on carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) hydrogel immobilized with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD) nanoparticles (NPs) for cutting-edge anticounterfeiting applications. Security inks that self-heal are very durable. Under ambient conditions, the prepared NCD@CMC hydrogel could self-heal with a high healing efficiency. It might stick to diverse surfaces such as plastic, glass and paper sheets. The self-healing composite ink demonstrated outstanding photostability under UV light. Straightforward and environmentally friendly method was applied on the agricultural waste of rice straw toward the production of NCD using hydrothermal carbonization in an aqueous medium, and in the presence of NH4OH as an inexpensive passivating agent. The quantum yield (QY) for NCD reached 24.09 %. Various concentrations of NCD NPs were employed to produce self-healable nanocomposite inks with a variety of emission properties. Stamping homogeneous films onto paper surfaces produced a transparent layer. The CIE Lab and emission spectra of prints independently verified the capability of NCD nanocomposite inks to vary their color to blue under UV illumination. To measure the particle diameter of the prepared NCD, their morphological characteristics were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to indicate diameters of 10–25 nm. Utilizing various analytical techniques, the morphology and chemical composition of the fluorescent prints were examined. We examined the mechanical qualities of the stamped papers as well as the rheological characteristics of the ink hydrogel. Due to their colorless appearance, the excitation band of the printed films was peaked at 364 nm, while their emission was peaked at 465 nm. The current smart ink holds high potential for numerous applications like smart packaging and authentication, and shows great promise as a practical and mass production approach for easily creating anticounterfeiting stamps.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rice straw-based carbon dots
Carboxymethyl cellulose
Fluorescence
Self-healing security hydrogel
Paper sheet

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2023R122), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Impact of NH4OH to CDA ratio on quantum yield of NCD.
Transmission electron microscopic images of NCD.
FTIR spectra of CDA (a), and NCD (b).

NCD6 hydrogel circular film with a ~ 1 mm thickness placed on a flat solid surface (a) was cut into two semicircles wetted at the interfaces (b) and brought near together (c) to demonstrate the self-healing process (d). The self-healed hydrogel circle was able to support a weight of 100 g (e). NCD6 circular film functioned as a stable vessel sealant for glass tubes containing colored distilled water (f), aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide (g), and aqueous solution of hydrochloric acid (h).
SEM images of NCD@CMC casted films; NCD0 (a, b), and NCD8 (c, d).
SEM analysis of stamped paper sheet; NCD6.
Excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra of NCD in aqueous medium.
Fluorescence spectra of NCD at different pH values.
Viscosity of NCD@CMC (NCD6) versus shearing rates.
Impacts of NCD ratio on mechanical activity of NCD@CMC prints.
Elemental ratios of NCD0 and NCD6 at three areas of sample surface.
Coloration screening of prints at different NCD concentrations under white light (WL) and UV (UV) illumination.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots-embedded fluorescent carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogel for anticounterfeiting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tabuk, 71474 Tabuk, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tabuk, 71474 Tabuk, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Applied Sciences, Umm Al-Qura University, Makkah, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Applied Sciences, Umm Al-Qura University, Makkah, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, P.O. Box 84428, Riyadh 11671, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, P.O. Box 84428, Riyadh 11671, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Mansoura University, Mansoura, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Mansoura University, Mansoura, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Applied Sciences, Umm Al-Qura University, Makkah, Saudi Arabia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent inks have been emerged as a desirable encoding technique to enhance anticounterfeiting printing of commercial goods. However, significant drawbacks with fluorescent inks, such as poor durability, low efficiency, and high cost. Herein, we describe the preparation of a self-healing authentication ink based on carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) hydrogel immobilized with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD) nanoparticles (NPs) for cutting-edge anticounterfeiting applications. Security inks that self-heal are very durable. Under ambient conditions, the prepared NCD@CMC hydrogel could self-heal with a high healing efficiency. It might stick to diverse surfaces such as plastic, glass and paper sheets. The self-healing composite ink demonstrated outstanding photostability under UV light. Straightforward and environmentally friendly method was applied on the agricultural waste of rice straw toward the production of NCD using hydrothermal carbonization in an aqueous medium, and in the presence of NH4OH as an inexpensive passivating agent. The quantum yield (QY) for NCD reached 24.09 %. Various concentrations of NCD NPs were employed to produce self-healable nanocomposite inks with a variety of emission properties. Stamping homogeneous films onto paper surfaces produced a transparent layer. The CIE Lab and emission spectra of prints independently verified the capability of NCD nanocomposite inks to vary their color to blue under UV illumination. To measure the particle diameter of the prepared NCD, their morphological characteristics were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to indicate diameters of 10–25 nm. Utilizing various analytical techniques, the morphology and chemical composition of the fluorescent prints were examined. We examined the mechanical qualities of the stamped papers as well as the rheological characteristics of the ink hydrogel. Due to their colorless appearance, the excitation band of the printed films was peaked at 364 nm, while their emission was peaked at 465 nm. The current smart ink holds high potential for numerous applications like smart packaging and authentication, and shows great promise as a practical and mass production approach for easily creating anticounterfeiting stamps.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

ce:section.ce:para: The counterfeit of banknotes, commercial merchandise and official certificates has been a global disaster with far-reaching security, economic, and political consequences. The underground economy sudden ascent in value to the multi-billion dollar level can be directly attributed to the proliferation of forgeries. As a result, developing innovative anti-counterfeiting tactics using advanced materials is an urgent need [1–3]. It has been suggested that anti-counterfeiting systems might benefit from the optical properties as a form of security authentication as those systems are easy to verify but difficult to imitate. Technologies such as plasmonic nanoparticles and quantum dots (QD) have been hailed as promising candidates for next-generation optically active anti-counterfeiting materials [4]. The high emission efficiency, tiny and homogeneous particle size, and other desirable properties of these materials make them attractive for application in a variety of anti-counterfeiting technologies [5]. But since their anti-counterfeiting optical characteristics rely so heavily on a relatively short fluorescence, printed samples like banknotes are subject to considerable interferences from background emission. In order to produce advanced anti-counterfeiting products free of fluorescence interferences in the background, innovative materials with long-lasting photoluminescence have become a need [6]. Security authentication has been emerged as a major requirement in various contexts, including banknotes, passports, certificates, and identification cards. As a result, several ink, digital and paper verification methods have been presented. Recent techniques for creating authentication patterns have been published, including radio frequency identification cards and holographic labels [7–9]. However, these techniques are costly and need complicated procedures. For this reason, anti-counterfeiting photochromic inks have been developed to print security patterns using a variety of materials such as upconversion nanoparticles and polymeric dots [10,11]. These materials were able to develop effective solvent and aqueous photochromic prints. Photochromism can be defined as a colorimetric transition between two different states with different absorption and/or emission spectra in response to input light. Potential applications for photochromism in smart materials include sensors, packaging, memory, and eyewear [12–14]. Photochromic polymer-based inks have been widely investigated owing to their optical properties and great stability. Polymer nanoparticle-based photochromic inks have excellent optical features owing to their high surface area and low light reflection. However, the printer nozzles may have serious issues if polymer nanoparticles cluster together [15]. The term “fluorescence” refers to an emission of light by a substance after that molecule has absorbed light [16]. This fluorescence emission ceases immediately upon removal of the excitation source. Photochromism is a reversible shift between two colorimetric states triggered by light [17].

Photoluminescent nanoparticles distributed in polymeric media have been shown to improve the efficacy of anti-counterfeiting applications [18]. Thus, there has been an extensive research on the development of photoluminescent nanomaterials for use in secure authentication and information encryption. Among these luminescent nanomaterials, carbon dots stood out as particularly intriguing nanoparticles due to their advantages of low toxicity, biocompatibility, simple preparation procedures, and exceptional optical properties [19]. Due to these characteristics, they are a preferable alternative to organic dyes. Carbon dots are formidable competitors as compared to semiconductor QD, which have sophisticated preparation procedures, low quantum yield (QY), high toxicity and high cost. Several potential uses for carbon dots have been proposed, including packaging, information encoding, detection of explosives, anticounterfeiting, cellular labeling, and switching device [20]. Carbon dots (CD) are nanoparticles that have a diameter of tens of nanometers or less and are roughly spherical in shape. They have been synthesized by the top-down technology using a carbon material bulk, or by the bottom-up method using precursor seeds to commence the creation of nanoparticle. In the bottom-up method, CD are mainly prepared from organic polymers, amino acids, and citric acid. Fluorescent CD can be prepared by various procedures, leading to varied QY [21]. Chattopadhyay and Sk [22] used citric acid to develop carbon dots were of uniform in size and had a fluorescent quantum yield of 73.5 %. Using electrothermal procedures at temperatures between 25 and 95 °C, Liu and coworkers [23] produced nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD) from citric acid with a 75 % quantum yield. Nitrogen doping improves the optical and thermal stability of CD. However, unlike the green emission of QD, reports indicate that NCD exhibit shifting emissions of intense blue color. It has also been noted that the blue shift emission is pH dependant. No one knows for sure what causes the red or blue changes, although the process may include the agglomeration of carbon dots or protonation. Khan et al. [24] used a solid-state reaction method to produce carbon dots with green emission from diammonium hydrogen citrate and urea, reporting a quantum yield of 46.4 %. Li et al. [25] found that nitrogen-doped carbon dots, when exposed to UV radiation, generate green light at ambient temperature. The raw components used to synthesize NCD were citric acid, urea, and acrylamide. Jiang and coworkers [26] developed multicolored CD, which emitted in a range of wavelengths from cyan to yellow phosphorescence and blue to green florescence when illuminated with UV light. Both fluorescence and phosphorescence can be employed to provide reliable and robust anticounterfeiting identification systems. Carbon dots with photochromic properties have recently been produced from plants [27]. Researchers led by Gao found that NCD extracted from willow leaves fluoresced bright blue when exposed to UV light at 356 nm. Their improved photostability under fluorescent conditions (0.02 % decay rate vs. 0.45 % for dyes like rhodamine) makes them a more desirable choice [28]. However, most anti-counterfeiting printed films have had drawbacks including poor durability. Consequently, there has been an uptick in interest to create self-healing anti-counterfeiting materials. Self-healing materials are resilient because they can repair themselves after being damaged [29]. Self-healing security inks have showed a vital role in the preparation of cutting-edge anti-counterfeiting applications [30]. Cellulose, from which carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is made, is said to be one of the most common renewable and natural resources. The three hallmarks of CMC are its low cost, biocompatibility, and biodegradability [31,32]. Because of its biocompatibility and self-healing properties, CMC has found use in a variety of fields such as tissue engineering and healthcare. CMC-based self-healable hydrogels are characterized by low mechanical strength and superabsorbent characteristics. Thus, CMC have been used as a composite ink that can be merged with NCD nanoparticles. CMC-based self-healable inks can be reported with high durability because of their self-healing properties [31,32]. To the best of our knowledge, no previous reports were presented on the preparation of self-healing and fluorescent NCD-doped CMC-based self-healable inks. The focus of this work is on coming up with a straightforward and environmentally friendly method of producing self-healable hydrogel immobilized with carbon dots from rice straw scraps. To create a nanocomposite ink with fluorescence and photochromism, NCD NPs were encapsulated in the self-healable CMC. Hydrogels made of CMC can cure themselves when doped with aluminum ions [33]. The photochromic effect was accomplished by including NCD into the CMC matrix. The capacity of CMC to self-heal can be ascribed to the reversible coordination bond formation of carboxylic substituents on CMC with aluminum ions. The carboxylate groups of CMC can efficiently begin strong ionic contacts with aluminum ions, allowing for the creation of dynamic ionic coordinative bonds necessary for the achievement of self-healing activity [34]. Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX), X-ray fluorescence (XRF), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), infrared spectroscopy (IR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were employed to inspect the morphologies of both NCD particles and stamped paper sheets. Additionally, the fluorescence spectra, mechanical characteristics, and CIE Lab parameters were explored. The mechanical features of the stamped paper samples as well as the ink rheological activity were studied. The anti-counterfeiting patterns fluoresced and darkened under UV light, demonstrating a dual-mode fluorescence and photochromic phenomena. To protect the economy and society from the dangers posed by counterfeit and subpar commercial items, the current research can be described as an effective approach toward mass manufacture of verified products.

The chemicals employed in the current investigation were acquired from commercial vendors, including sodium hydroxide (NaOH; Merck, Egypt), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO; Sigma-Aldrich, Egypt), hydrochloric acid (HCl; Merck, Egypt), sulfuric acid (H2SO4; Merck, Egypt), sodium chlorite (NaClO2; Merck, Egypt), and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH; Aldrich, Egypt). Merck (Egypt) was contacted for the acquisition of tetrabutylammonium acetate (TBAA) and acetic anhydride. Aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3 .6H2O; AR) and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) sodium salt (Mw = 250,000 Da; 1500–3100 mPa s) were purchased from. The prepared NCD@CMC hydrogel ink was used to print various designs onto the surface of Whatman sheets (off-white) obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Egypt). Whatman paper exhibited a thickness of 180 μm, diameter 240 mm, pore size of 11 μm, and weight 87 g/m2.

The source of the rice straws was Sharqia Governorate, Egypt. Following methods previously published [35], CDA was synthesized from rice straw. NaOH(aq) (17.5 %; w/v) was mixed with rice straws (~5 cm in length) for 2 h. After being soaked in water, the bloated straws were washed and mixed for 30 min before being dried in the open air. Sodium hydroxide enters the amorphous zone of plant cell walls, disrupting the intermolecular interactions between the molecules. By hydrolyzing the resultant mass with a diluted HCl solution, we were able to remove the hemicelluloses, extractive components, and amorphous cellulose. With a constant stirrer speed, we carried out 80 °C hydrolysis treatments of varied acid ratios (1, 2 and 3 mol) and times (1, 2 and 3 h). The bulk pH was neutralized by washing with water and air-dried. After being hydrolyzed, the bulk was rinsed with distilled water and dried after being subjected to NaOH(aq) (2 %; w/v) for 2 h at 85 °C under constant stirring. The existence of insoluble lignin rendered the sample brown even after alkaline treatment. In order to accomplish the bleaching process, the sample was mixed with NaClO2(aq) (20 %; w/v) at 55 °C for an hour. The amount of sodium chlorite required was roughly calculated using the kappa number of the fibers. After bleaching, the fibers were washed and air-dried. To fibrillate nanofibers, a cylindrical probe was used to homogenize (20 KHz; 400 W) cellulose fiber slurry (1 % wt) for 30 min in a 125 mL of distilled water. The sonication method was performed in an ice bath to maintain a constant temperature. Following a previously described methods [35], cellulose diacetate with a degree of substitution of 2.65 was introduced. The extracted cellulose was slowly added to a solvent combination of TBAA/DMSO (1:4). The admixture was stirred at 60 °C for 30 min to get a colorless solution. Then, the aqueous solution was treated to a gradual addition of acetic anhydride. The solution was heated to 60 °C and stirred for 3 h before being precipitated by methanol. The produced solution was filtered under vacuum, rinsed with methanol, and vacuum-dried to provide CDA.

NCD were synthesized using a hydrothermal method [36]. After many washes in water, the rice straw extract needed to be dried at 80 °C for an hour. The produced rice straw extract (0.2 g) was mixed with NH4OH (0.1 M), and the resulting solution were diluted to 20 mL with distilled water. Heating the mixtures for 15 h at 240 °C in a Teflon-lined, 50 mL autoclave was applied. After the reaction was finished, the autoclave was exposed to cooling to ambient conditions. After 10 min of centrifugation at 10,000 rpm, the black precipitates had been separated out of the admixtures. The provided sample was purified by filtration using an ultrafine filtration system (0.22 μm). Impurities were removed by filtrate dialysis (dialysis tube, MWCO 1000) for 24 h against de-ionized water. NCD were generated and stored at 4 °C for further study.

Using mechanical stirring for 2 h, we were able to create a homogenous suspension of CMC (6 % w/v) in distilled water that had the appearance of a translucent paste [37]. A 15 wt% aqueous solution of aluminum chloride hexahydrate (0.5 mL) was added to the CMC solution (5 mL). Solutions with 0 %, 0.05 %, 0.1 %, 0.25 %, 0.5 %, 0.75 %, 1 %, 1.25 %, and 1.5 % w/w of NCD NPs were produced. For uniform dispersion, we mixed each combination for 60 min and then homogenized at 25 kHz using an ultrasonic sonicator (Hielscher Company, Teltow, Germany) for 30 min. In order to produce the CMC-based hydrogel, each solution was allowed to settle under ambient conditions for 24 h. The prepared NCD@CMC hydrogel inks were used to print different patterns onto Whatman paper surface using a wood stamp. The prints were air-dried for 20 min. The substrates that were imprinted were labeled with symbols ranging from NCD0 to NCD8, respectively that corresponded to the NCD NPs ratio. We also made NCD@CMC translucent films by pouring the aforementioned solutions onto Teflon Petri plates and letting them to air-dry for 24 h.

SEM Quanta FEG-250 (Czech Republic) paired with EDX for element mapping was used to inspect the drop-casted films and stamping sheet morphologies. Bruker Advance D8 was utilized to get the XRD spectrum of NCD NPs (Germany). The morphological characteristics of NCD nanoparticles were analyzed by JEOL 1230 (Japan). With the use of infrared spectral analysis using a Nexus 670 (Nicolet, USA), we were able to learn about the functional groups present in the NCD@CMC hydrogel structure. Using a Brookfield model DV-III rheometer, the hydrogel apparent viscosity was measured at several shear rates to learn more about its rheological characteristics. The mechanical performance of paper substrates immobilized with different concentrations of NCD NPs was measured using a Zwick universal (Germany). The absorption spectra were calculated using a UV1006M031 spectrometer (China). The excitation and emission spectra were investigated using a JASCO FP-6500 (Japan). Using quinine sulfate (standardized solution) with a QY of 54 % in a solution of H2SO4 (0.1 M), Eq. (1) was employed to compute QY of NCD at 365 nm [38].
(1)
QY =
I /
I r
×
A r
/ A
×
η 2
/
η r
2
where QY represents quantum yield; η is the refraction index of solvents; r is the standardized sample; I is the emission intensity; and A is the absorbance intensity.

The hydrogel ink photostability was determined according to previous procedures [39]. After the stamping process was completed, the printed sheet was placed four centimetres below the UV light source. The paper substrate was illuminated by ultraviolet for 5 min at 365 nm, and the emission spectra were then reported. The preceding steps were repeated many cycles to indicate high photostability.

The colorimetric shifts of prints were investigated by UltraScanPro (HunterLab, United States) to measure CIE Lab and color strength (K/S) before and after UV irradiation [40]. The CIE Lab coordinates, including L⁎, a⁎, and b⁎ indicate the range from white (100) to black (0), green (−a⁎) to red (+a⁎), and blue (−b⁎) to yellow (+b⁎), respectively. Canon A710IS photos of a stamp sheet were taken under both UV and regular lights.

According to previous procedures [35], the cytotoxicity of the NCD@CMC hydrogel was examined utilizing a cell counting kit-8 assay (Sigma-Aldrich, Egypt). Mouse skin cells (Msc; Sigma-Aldrich, Egypt) were grown in a carbon dioxide incubator (5 %) at 24.2 °C for 24 h on a 96-well plate with Dulbecco's modified eagle media (DMEM; Sigma-Aldrich, Egypt). Msc was exposed to incubation for 24 h at 24.3 °C and carbon dioxide (5 %) after the NCD@CMC hydrogel was introduced to the DMEM. NCD@CMC was used to replace the DMEM medium at concentrations of 0, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, and 150 g/mL. For a further 24 h, the cells were exposed to incubation at 24.3 °C and 5 % carbon dioxide. After the DMEM solution was removed, CCK-8 was included, and the well was exposed to incubation at 37 °C in carbon dioxide (5 %) for 4 h. The optical density of the cell was determined at 490 nm. Since the incubation conditions for the infected and uninfected Msc were identical, the uninfected Msc were used as controls. The following equation [35] was used to infer the viability of Msc cells, representing cytotoxicity.
(2)
Msc
viability
%
=
N D
/
N T
× 100
%
where, NT is the control absorbance; and ND is Msc at various contents of NCD@CMC.

Off-white (unbleached) sheets of Whatman cellulose paper were printed by different patterns using a wooden stamp. When exposed to UV light, the printed images emitted a bright blue fluorescence. The current NCD@CMC hydrogel has the potential to print security patterns for the purposes of information encryption owing to its positive characteristics such as cheapness, ease of usage, simple preparation, and being environmentally friendly. Blank paper revealed an off-white colorimetric shade as a slightly dark background that improves the printed sheet optical photochromic perception to blue. Under ultraviolet light, the resultant blue hue can be clearly detected by naked-eye on a dark surface like the current off-white sheets. Under UV light, the prints exhibited instantaneous and reversible photochromism.

Different contents of NCD NPs were incorporated into a homogenous aqueous solution of CMC and aqueous solution of aluminum chloride hexahydrate. Transparency of the hydrogel film is dependent on the NCD NPs being evenly distributed throughout the CMC solution. The NCD@CMC hydrogel was imprinted onto paper substrates using a wooden stamp. A hydrogel capacity to self-heal increases its resistance to mechanical injury [33,34]. Under ultraviolet illumination, the blue emission of NCD6 was easily observed. With the humidity at 90 %, two NCD6 hydrogel semicircles were brought near together as shown in Fig. 5a-d. The healing process occurred by the hydrogel on its own at room temperature. The two sides of the hydrogel slowly fused together, and the semicircles healed with each other. A total of 100 g was successfully supported by the complete mended ring as shown in Fig. 5e. Multiple H-bonds formed between CMC hydroxyl groups contribute to the self-healing activity, as does coordination bonding between the carboxylic anions and aluminum ions [33,34]. To speed up the repair process, H-bonds were formed with the help of humidity across the injured contact. The current REA@CMC hydrogel (NCD6) has the potential to function as a stable vessel sealant for glass tubes containing colored distilled water, aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide, and aqueous solution of hydrochloric acid (Fig. 5f-h). The REA@CMC hydrogel immediately stopped the leak of an aqueous solution when used to seal a glass vial or plastic tube. The key point of the present approach is to develop CMC-based self-healable hydrogel integrated with NCD NPs. Fig. 6 displays SEM images of NCD-integrated casted films, revealing a smooth surface like NCD-free film.

In recent years, anticounterfeiting prints have received increased interest owing to their adaptable structure and possible applications, including biological imaging and sensors. Fig. 7 shows the SEM analysis of fluorescent printed film (NCD6). SEM images confirmed that a uniform covering of NCD@CMC had been deposited onto the paper sheet. These photographs demonstrated that NCD@CMC particles were evenly dispersed throughout the cellulose sheet using the printing deposition method. Due to their nano-scaled nature, the NCD@CMC particles were found to be evenly distributed over the cellulose surface. There were no discernible alterations between the treated and untreated document surfaces in terms of the cellulose fiber structure, as shown in the SEM images of paper sheets. Elements present in NCD6 were analyzed using EDX at different points on sheet surface and are summarised in Table 1 . Scanning the NCD@CMC-printed documents at three separate sites revealed that their elemental composition was virtually unchanged. The doping process was further proved by the presence of higher contents of sulfur and nitrogen in NCD as compared to CDA. No sulfur compounds, except for CDA, were used in the preparation process of NCD@CMC. On the other hand, nitrogen was mainly sourced from the passivation agent (MH4OH), whereas CDA is the source of carbon.

The development of carbon dots with dimensions below 100 nm has garnered much attention as a novel kind of nano-scaled structures. Carbon dots have shown great promise as a new category of fluorescent agents owing to their low cost, non-toxicity, and biocompatibility [18–21]. Condensation, cross-linking, and carbonization are said to produce CD, which are then recognized as microscopic molecular entities or polymers. Thus, CD often have a structure that is a hybrid of carbon core and fluorescent polymeric structures. These unique structures help the CD maintain their high fluorescence quantum yield and high water solubility. Research into the design of CD various applications such as catalysis, sensing and bioimaging, has been reported in several publications [20,21]. Hydrothermal treatment of biomass material, like lignin, cellulose, hemicellulose, starch and chitosan, has garnered high attention among other methods due to its renewability, cheapness, and environmentally friendly properties. These biomass precursors; however, have repeatedly shown their worth as resources in a wide range of manufacturing applications. Therefore, it is still highly desirable to use these biomass precursors to create high-quality CD as replacement substrates for other costly industrial products [41]. To create a fluorescent CD-based film out of rice straw, we opted for a method that was both economical and benign to the environment. As a biomass that contains a lot of cellulous, rice straw is readily available. Preparation of carbon dots using cellulosic biomass has been demonstrated to be feasible [35]. Hydrothermal curing in the presence of N-based passivation agent was required to create NCD. In order to make a film out of the fluorescent carbon dots, a gel containing the dots was first created by coupling them to a transparent polymer. Using both visible and UV light, the nanocomposite film was evaluated for its photochromic emission. In order to verify blue emission, the UV mode was switched on. The possibilities presented by the development of synthetic photochromic carbon dots are intriguing. Due to the ease with which carbon dots can be synthesized, dual mode fluorescent emission inks can be manufactured in huge quantities. There is a wide variety of fluorescent-based applications that might benefit from using agricultural waste in the manufacture of fluorescent CD [42]. The prepared NCD exhibited remarkable optical properties. The NCD aqueous solution was yellow in visible spectrum but emitted blue when illuminated with UV (365 nm). The absorbance spectra of NCD showed a single peak at 364 nm, which were identified as being caused by CC bond formation (π-π ⁎ transition) and CO bond formation (n-π ⁎ transition). The absorbance at 364 nm was monitored to be optimal for the NCD emission at 465 nm. The calculated quantum yield was monitored at 24.09 %. Once the excitation was shifted by 15 nm, from 364 to 379 nm, the fluorescence band of NCD bathochromically shifted and their intensity reduced. This excitation-dependent fluorescence activity has been previously reported [35–37]. The emission stability of NCD in aqueoua media was studied to identify the effects of pH and irradiation duration. Fig. 8 shows the results of analyzing both excitation and emission spectra. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots displayed remarkable increased fluorescence upon increasing the pH value from 2 to 7 as shown in Fig. 9 . NCD emitted at their brightest in neutral solutions (pH 7). When the pH was changed from 7 to 12 or from 7 to 2, the emission intensity of NCD decreased because of the protonation-deprotonation mechanism of functional groups on NCD surface. Accordingly, it was hypothesized that the NCD wouldn't react to the somewhat acidic environment. The fluorescence band of NCD moved to a bluer wavelength when the pH increased from 2 to 12. Protective shell, carbon dot aggregation, protonation-deprotonation, energy level shift and self-aggregation are all plausible mechanisms proposed to explain the pH-responsive behavior of NCD [35,36]. A definitive explanation underlying the emission behavior of NCD has not yet been identified yet. For instance, the ability to precisely assess the structural-optical interaction is hampered by the lack of a validated CD structure. It was challenging to draw results due to the fact that carbon dots might exhibit different pH-dependent emission while being produced using the identical precursors or procedures. Carbon dots fabricated from graphite rods emitted in a redder spectrum as reported by Yuan [43], but CD fabricated by Fan from the same precursors emitted bluer light as reported by Fan [44]. Other research groups used the same hydrothermal technique to create carbon dots out of citric acid and glucose [45,46]. However, the emission properties of these two types of carbon dots rely on the surrounding pH. When exposed to UV light for 60 min, the NCD hardly changed in fluorescence intensity, demonstrating their remarkable photostability. This could be ascribed to the carboxylic groups found on NCD surface.

The fatigue resistance testing results for the NCD@CMC security ink printed sheet are shown in Fig. 10 under a variety of daylight and UV exposure circumstances. The fluorescent film (NCD6) did quite well with regard to its fatigue resistance. The emission spectra were collected for several cycles of illumination with UV radiation for 5 min. The fluorescent prints were found to be very durable, photostable, and reversible, with the emission intensity remaining almost constant after each UV illumination cycle.

The capacity to alter the color of the printed papers was evaluated using the outcomes of the coloring process. Table 2 shows the results of CIE Lab parameters used to assess the photo-switchable printed ink, which included testing for reversibility and photostability. The translucent look of the printed film was shown by the off-white color of the immaculate and treated sheets. K/S rose with increasing the NCD ratio under both white and UV lights, showing that K/S had improved. The K/S value rose marginally with increasing the NCD ratio in daylight, indicating the creation of a dimmer yellow, and rose dramatically with increasing NCD ratio under UV radiation, indicating the formation of a deeper blue shade. Upon increasing the NCD ratio under white light, there was no discernible change in the values of L⁎, a⁎ and b⁎ for the stamped papers in comparison to the off-white blank paper. Due to the low concentration of NCD in the hydrogel matrix, there were no changes in K/S either before or after stamping, demonstrating its transparent nature as a photochromic film. The findings revealed that the optimal amount of NCD for coloring was at a ratio of 1 %. Under white light, increasing the ratio of NCD had no effect on –a⁎ or + b⁎; however, it did have a small effect on L⁎, indicating that a colorless ink layer was used to coat the printed paper sheets. Under UV light, both –a⁎ and –b⁎ rose, whereas L⁎ dropped, indicating the formation a blue color.

The findings of MTT tests [35] showing the cytotoxicity of NCD@CMC against MSc in DMEM with different dosages of NCD@CMC applied for 24 h are displayed in Fig. 11 . The viability of Msc cells was shown to be unaffected by the NCD@CMC contents up to 150 g/mL. NCD@CMC is non-toxic when utilized on healthy cell lines, suggesting their potential use as an optical probe for anticounterfeiting purposes ranging from currency to food packaging.

The rheological properties of the fluorescent NCD@CMC hydrogel security ink (NCD5) are shown in Fig. 12 . The NCD@CMC hydrogel is considered viscous since the measured flow rate obeyed a non-Newtonian law [47]. As the shearing rate was increased, the perceived viscosity decreased rapidly and linearly. Evidence showed that the apparent viscosity rapidly decreased to attain equilibrium at higher shearing speeds.

The NCD@CMC inks were stamped onto paper sheets for examination of their mechanical performance. Young's modulus, tensile strength and strain% of prints at different NCD concentrations are shown in Fig. 13 . The tensile and Young's modulus were observed to increase with raising NCD concentration from NCD0 to NCD5, with relatively minor variations monitored for NCD5 to NCD8. Strain at break % hardly changed when NCD content went from NCD0 to NCD8.

Printed patterns were prepared from a dual-mode photochromic self-healable hydrogel and applied onto paper substrates, causing a transition in color from off-white to blue under UV light, as confirmed by emission spectra and CIE Lab coloration measurements. Smart ink with high photostability and endurance was developed by combining NCD nanoparticles as a photochromic agent with CMC as a self-healing hydrogel agent. The optimal blue emission was shown by the NCD@CMC print with NCD ratio of 1 %. In response to a 364 nm excitation, the NCD@CMC prints displayed an emission peak at 465 nm. The transparency of the stamped film was improved due to the even distribution of NCD nanoparticles (10–25 nm) in CMC. The present colorless photochromic hydrogel can be used as smart ink on a wide range of surfaces for low-cost, self-healing, and effective authentication. Rice straw waste was effectively used to develop useful nitrogen-doped carbon dots nanoparticles. An aqueous solution of NH4OH was applied as a passivating agent in a hydrothermal procedure as a cheap and nontoxic method for the preparation of NCD from rice straw agricultural waste. The emission spectra of NCD were explored, demonstrating a quantum yield as high as 24.09 %. Only negligible differences were observed in the mechanical behavior of prints when increasing NCD ratio. The hydrogel viscosity, photostability and toxicity were explored, displaying excellent characteristics without toxic effects.
All authors agreed on their roles and the order of names in the manuscript and this on behalf of them.
Not applicable.
The author(s) agree to publish the article under the Creative Commons Attribution License.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2023R122), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
